title: Mariska Hargitay Explores Her Mother's Legacy in New Film
content:
Veteran actor and advocate Mariska Hargitay, known to millions as Captain Olivia Benson on Law and Order: Special Victims Unit, recently stepped into a new role as a documentary filmmaker. With her feature-film directorial debut, 'My Mom Jayne', Hargitay examined the life, career, and complicated legacy of her mother. Her mother was 1950s Hollywood starlet Jayne Mansfield, who died in a 1967 car accident when Hargitay was just three years old. For most of her life, Hargitay felt conflicted about her mother's movie persona, which often capitalized on the “dumb blonde” stereotype. However, making the documentary gave her a chance to see Mansfield as a multidimensional woman who was a classically trained violinist and spoke five languages.

As much as the film is a love letter to her mother, it was also a profound journey of self-discovery for Hargitay. In the documentary, she reveals that the man who raised her, Mickey Hargitay, was not her biological father. Instead, she discovered at age 25 that singer Nelson Sardelli was her true biological father. She kept this secret for over 30 years to protect Mickey, whom she deeply loved and regarded as her true guiding star. Sharing this part of her family’s story became a way to invite others to look at their own families and see the love that can emerge from the truth.

This need for connection and healing has driven Hargitay’s advocacy work for nearly half her life. In 2004, she started the Joyful Heart Foundation to help survivors of sexual and domestic abuse find justice through legislation reform and community building. Recently, she helped pass the Take It Down Act, a federal law designed to combat the publication of nonconsensual intimate images online. In 2024, Hargitay also revealed she is a survivor of sexual assault, realizing that sharing her vulnerability acts as a powerful connector stitching humanity together. Ultimately, her vulnerability has become her greatest superpower both on and off the screen.
